text: Laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with complete mesocolic excision for a sequel tumor with associated phlegmon. A sixty-seven years old female, previously fit and well, presented to our emergency department with a two-week history of lower abdominal pain, localizing more to right lower quadrant. In view of the elevated inflammatory markers and anemia, a CT abdomen was organized. This showed marked inflammatory changes and fat stranding in the right iliac fossa. There were two small collections adjacent to terminal ileum and anterior abdominal wall with local lymphadenopathy. The ileocecal valve and the terminal ileum appeared thickened. However, the appendix could not be identified. The differential diagnosis included either endocytosis with abscess formation or a cecal tumor with focal perforation. The patient was successfully managed with intravenous antibiotics. Her symptoms improved and a repeat CT scan four days later showed improvement in the inflammation. The patient was discharged on day five with a prolonged course of antibiotics. She subsequently underwent a colonoscopy, which unfortunately showed a bulky eight centimeter cecal tumor involving the entire cecal pole and the ileocecal valve. Histopathology confirmed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma.
Staging CT was negative for distance metastasis. The patient subsequently proceeded to a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon induces pneumoperitoneum and observes the cecum firmly adherent to the anterior lateral abdominal wall. Using harmonic shears, the omental adhesions are divided, leaving the cecum end block. Dissection begins by identifying the ileocolic pedicle, creating a window near the mesenteric root between the ileocolic vessels and the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic vein is dissected down to its junction with the superior mesenteric vein, isolated with a right angle instrument and Maryland forceps, and clipped at its confluence. The ileocolic artery, passing anterior to the vein, is double clipped at its origin from the superior mesenteric artery, ensuring high ligation of ileocolic vessels for optimal oncological outcomes.

The surgeon dissects the ascending colonic mesentery in a cranial direction using a medial to lateral approach. The right colic artery is identified and divided, followed by division of the distal small bowel mesentery using harmonic shears. The small bowel is transected with an echelon-sixty stapler, 25 centimeters from the ileocecal valve. The cecal mass is carefully separated from the anterior lateral abdominal wall superiorly and the right fallopian tube and external iliac vessels inferiorly. A small peri-appendicular abscess is found and aspirated. The surgeon resects a segment of parietal peritoneum and lateral abdominal wall, removing them en bloc with the specimen. Dissection continues in the retroperitoneal plane, preserving the right ureter. The hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon are completely mobilized, with care taken to avoid injury to the duodenum and head of the pancreas.

The surgeon divides the colon between the proximal and mid-transverse sections. The specimen is extracted through a nine-centimeter incision with partial division of the rectus muscles. After closing the incision and re-establishing pneumoperitoneum, an isoperistaltic ileocolic side-to-side anastomosis is performed. The anastomosis is ensured to be well vascularized and tension-free. Two stay sutures are placed, and enterotomies are made using hook diathermy and Maryland forceps in both the ileum and colon. The anastomosis is completed with an echelon-sixty stapler, and the enterotomy is closed in two layers: the first with interrupted Vicryl stitches and the second with running sutures using self-locking suture.

A fifteen French plate drain is placed in the right iliac fossa at the end of the procedure. The drain output is milky white, indicating a potential chyle leak, which is managed conservatively. The drain is removed on post-operative day twelve.

Overall staging was of T-four, N-one-C, V-one, L-one, P-N-one, R-O. It has been suggested that complete mesocholic excision in right hemicolectomy might lead to improved oncological outcomes, as it yields higher number of resected lymph nodes and a better tumor clearance margin. Laparoscopic approach to right hemicolectomy with CME can be challenging due to variability in vascular anatomy. However, in experienced hands, it is feasible and safe, resulting in a more extensive lymphadenectomy and a better oncological radicality.
